# 🏥 ML Project — Health Insurance Premium Prediction

## 📌 Project Overview

This project demonstrates an end-to-end machine learning pipeline built to predict annual health insurance premiums, starting from raw data analysis to a fully deployed real-time prediction web application.

The primary goal is to predict the annual health insurance premium (₹) for a customer based on personal, financial, and medical information such as age, income, BMI, smoking status, medical history, and insurance plan type.

The project is designed to closely resemble real-world ML systems, covering:

- Exploratory Data Analysis and data cleaning
- Feature engineering and preprocessing
- Model training, evaluation, and hyperparameter tuning
- Age-based model segmentation for improved accuracy
- Interactive prediction interface using Streamlit
- Production-grade UI with custom CSS styling

---

## 🌐 Live Demo

**👉 Try the app here → [coversmart-healthcare-premium-prediction.streamlit.app](https://coversmart-healthcare-premium-prediction.streamlit.app/)**

---

---

## 📂 Project Structure

```
ml-project-premium-prediction/
│
├── artifacts/
│   ├── model_rest.joblib        → Trained XGBoost model for age > 25
│   ├── model_young.joblib       → Trained XGBoost model for age ≤ 25
│   ├── scaler_rest.joblib       → Feature scaler for age > 25
│   └── scaler_young.joblib      → Feature scaler for age ≤ 25
│
├── main.py                      → Streamlit web application (UI logic)
├── prediction_helper.py         → Feature preprocessing & prediction logic
├── style.css                    → Custom CSS for premium website styling
├── requirements.txt             → Python dependencies
└── README.md
```

---

## 1️⃣ Data Collection & Cleaning

- Dataset contains **50,000 records** with 13 features covering personal, financial, and health attributes
- Identified and handled **3 columns with missing values**: `smoking_status`, `employment_status`, `income_level`
- Missing values handled using **mode imputation**
- Detected multicollinearity — `income_level` (r = 0.91 with `income_lakhs`) was dropped to prevent model bias

---

## 2️⃣ Feature Engineering

- Engineered `normalized_risk_score` from raw medical history strings using a custom disease-based risk scoring table
- Applied **Label Encoding** for ordinal features (e.g., `insurance_plan`)
- Applied **One-Hot Encoding** for nominal features (e.g., `gender`, `region`, `bmi_category`, `smoking_status`)
- Discovered strong age-based non-linearity in premium patterns → led to **dual-model architecture** (age ≤ 25 vs age > 25)

---

## 3️⃣ Model Training & Evaluation

| Model | R² Score | RMSE |
|---|---|---|
| Linear Regression (Baseline) | 92.84% | ₹2,247 |
| XGBoost — Default | 98.07% | ₹1,165 |
| XGBoost — Tuned | **98.19%** | **₹1,130** |

- Achieved **R² of 98.19%** — exceeding the client target of 97%
- Reduced RMSE by **49.7%** compared to the Linear Regression baseline
- CV R² ≈ Test R² across both models — **no overfitting confirmed**

---

## 4️⃣ Hyperparameter Tuning

Two-step tuning approach for robust optimisation:

```
Step 1 — RandomizedSearchCV
  Search space  : 18,000+ combinations
  Sampled       : 50 (150 fits, 3-fold cross validation)

Step 2 — GridSearchCV
  Combinations  : 27 (81 fits, 3-fold cross validation)
  Purpose       : Fine-grained search around best region
```

Best Parameters found:

```python
XGBRegressor(
    n_estimators     = 300,
    learning_rate    = 0.05,
    max_depth        = 4,
    subsample        = 0.8,
    colsample_bytree = 1.0,
    min_child_weight = 7,
    random_state     = 42
)
```

---

## 5️⃣ Feature Importance

Top features driving premium predictions (by correlation with target):

```
Insurance Plan         ████████████████████  0.83
Age                    █████████████████░░░  0.77
Normalised Risk Score  ██████████░░░░░░░░░░  0.52
Marital Status         ██████████░░░░░░░░░░ -0.52
Number of Dependants   ████████░░░░░░░░░░░░  0.41
```

---

## 6️⃣ Web Application

The Streamlit app provides a real-world insurance website experience:

- **Navbar** — CoverSmart branding with navigation links and Get Free Quote CTA
- **Hero section** — full-width split layout with family image and ML stat bar (98% accuracy, 2 models, 12 features, <1s prediction)
- **4-step input form** — Personal Details, Employment & Region, Health Profile, Insurance Plan
- **Instant ML prediction** — routes to the correct age-segmented model automatically
- **Result panel** — displays annual premium, monthly breakdown, and a full factor summary with colour-coded chips
- **Custom CSS UI** — coral-mesh gradient background, SVG icon stat bar, plan cards with feature lists

Input features accepted by the app:

| Category | Features |
|---|---|
| Personal | Age, Gender, Marital Status, Number of Dependants |
| Financial | Annual Income (Lakhs), Employment Status, Insurance Plan |
| Health | BMI Category, Smoking Status, Medical History, Genetical Risk Score |
| Location | Region (Northwest, Southeast, Northeast, Southwest) |

---

## 🚀 How To Run Locally

```bash
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/Junead04/ml-project-premium-prediction.git
cd ml-project-premium-prediction

# 2. Create and activate a virtual environment
python -m venv venv

# Windows
venv\Scripts\activate

# Mac / Linux
source venv/bin/activate

# 3. Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# 4. Run the Streamlit app
streamlit run main.py
```

Open **http://localhost:8501** in your browser.

Or use the **live deployed version** directly:
**[https://coversmart-healthcare-premium-prediction.streamlit.app/](https://coversmart-healthcare-premium-prediction.streamlit.app/)**

---

## 🛠️ Tech Stack

| Category | Technology |
|---|---|
| Language | Python 3.10+ |
| ML Framework | XGBoost, Scikit-learn |
| Data Processing | Pandas, NumPy |
| Web Application | Streamlit |
| Visualisation | Matplotlib, Seaborn |
| Model Saving | Joblib |
| UI Styling | Custom CSS, Google Fonts |
| Deployment | Streamlit Community Cloud |

---

## 🧠 Key Learnings

- **Dual-model segmentation** — splitting data by age (≤ 25 vs > 25) significantly reduced prediction error compared to a single global model
- **Multicollinearity matters** — catching `income_level` (r = 0.91) early via correlation heatmap prevented misleading feature importance scores
- **Risk score engineering** — converting free-text medical history into a single normalised numeric score was the highest-impact feature engineering decision
- **Learning rate vs tree count tradeoff** — `learning_rate = 0.05` with 300 trees generalised better than `0.1` with 100 trees at similar accuracy
- **Two-step tuning** — RandomizedSearchCV to explore broadly, then GridSearchCV to refine, is far more efficient than GridSearch alone on large parameter spaces

---
